questions.question: Define wait() and notify() methods in Java.
keyPoints: wait(): releases the lock and makes the thread wait until notified
notify(): wakes up one waiting thread chosen by JVM
Both methods must be called from within a synchronized block or method

questions.question: What is the purpose of using wait() in a while loop?
keyPoints: To ensure the condition is met before proceeding
To avoid incorrect assumptions about the state of the shared resource

questions.question: Compare and contrast the wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() methods in Java.
keyPoints: wait(): releases the lock and makes the thread wait until notified
notify(): wakes up one waiting thread chosen by JVM
notifyAll(): wakes up all waiting threads, which then compete for the lock
Discussion on when to use each method, including scenarios where multiple threads are waiting for the same condition

questions.question: Discuss the importance of handling spurious wakeups in interthread communication.
keyPoints: Why wait() should always be used in a while loop to handle spurious wakeups
Example code snippet demonstrating the handling of spurious wakeups
Consequences of not handling spurious wakeups, including incorrect assumptions about the state of the shared resource

questions.question: Explain the Producer-Consumer problem and its solution using interthread communication in Java.
keyPoints: Explanation of how interthread communication can be used to solve the problem
Example code snippet demonstrating the use of wait() and notify() to coordinate the producer and consumer threads
Discussion on the benefits and limitations of using interthread communication in this scenario
